items.q: What is the difference between in-situ and ex-situ conservation?
items.correct: In-situ protects species in their natural habitat; ex-situ removes them to controlled settings like zoos or seed banks.
items.misconception: They are alternative names for the same approach.
items.random_wrong: One is for plants, the other for animals.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably. Each has trade-offs: in-situ preserves ecosystems; ex-situ provides safety net but loses evolutionary context. Most conservation strategies combine both.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.q: The Arabian oryx programme is significant because:
items.correct: It restored a species extinct in the wild via captive breeding
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It restored a species extinct in the wild via captive breeding. “It eradicated invasive species” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Select breeding pairs that minimise inbreeding
items.why: It's Select breeding pairs that minimise inbreeding. “Track migration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a captive population has 30 founders contributing equally, the mean inbreeding coefficient after one generation of random mating is ≈ 1/(2N). Calculate (3 d.p.).
items.why: The real figure is 0.017. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
